data.title: SLB on track to record seventh straight session of decline
data.description:
SLB N.V. (SLB) continued to log losses for the seventh straight session on Thursday. The stock was down 1.73% to $47.06 during midday trading.

The stock declined nearly 7% between February 26 and March 4. SLB ended 5.25% on March 3, further pressuring the share price.

However, on a YTD basis, the company has far outpaced the broader markets, gaining over 22% compared to a marginal movement in the S&P 500 index.

Last month, the company won two major contracts from the Kuwait Oil Company and Mubadala Energy in Indonesia. The Kuwaiti contract was worth $1.5B.

As per Seeking Alpha’s quant rating, the stock has a Hold rating with a score of 3.23 out of 5. SLB has been rated an A- for profitability, while it scored a D- for valuation. Seeking Alpha analysts also help with a cautious stance with a Hold rating. However, Wall Street analysts had a Buy call on the stock.

In January, SA analyst Dair Sansyzbayev rated the stock as a buy ahead of its earnings, arguing that excessive optimism was already baked into its stock price.

However, despite being overvalued, Sansyzbayev anticipated sentiment-driven share price spikes on the back of potentially positive news related to Venezuela.

data.title: Burlington Stores raises 2026 comp guidance to 1%-3% as tariff pressures ease and new store growth accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Burlington Stores (BURL) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Michael O'Sullivan, CEO, reported that "total sales increased 11%. This was on top of 10% total sales growth last year... we are continuing to take retail market share." He highlighted that comparable store sales grew 4%, exceeding the guidance range, and driven by the company's elevation strategy focused on better brands and higher quality assortments at strong value. O'Sullivan noted, "these trends drove a mid-single-digit increase in our average unit retail in the fourth quarter."
  • O'Sullivan explained that while the company was pleased with comp growth, tariff-driven assortment gaps limited results in certain key categories, particularly in home, toys, gifting, and housewares. He emphasized that actions taken in response to tariffs—such as reducing inventory in impacted categories and aggressive expense management—delivered "100 basis points of operating margin expansion and 21% earnings per share growth" in Q4.
  • For full-year 2025, Burlington delivered 9% total sales growth, 2% comp sales growth, 80 basis points of operating margin expansion, and 22% earnings per share growth. O'Sullivan stated, "we are very pleased with our results."
  • The CEO described a "bullish" outlook for 2026, citing resilient customers, a favorable tax refund season, and industry adjustment to tariffs. He added, "we are raising our comp guidance to 1% to 3% for the full year," and highlighted plans to aggressively pursue opportunities in the home category.
  • Kristin Wolfe, CFO, reported, "total sales grew 11% and comp store sales grew 4%, well above the high end of our guidance." She stated, "Q4 adjusted EBIT margin was 12.1%, and our adjusted earnings per share in Q4 was $4.99." Wolfe also elaborated on inventory strategy, liquidity position, and share repurchases, noting $2.2 billion in total liquidity and $385 million remaining on the share repurchase authorization.

Outlook

  • Burlington forecasts total sales growth of 8% to 10% for 2026, with 110 net new store openings planned. The company projects full-year comp store sales to increase 1% to 3% and adjusted EBIT margin to be flat to up 20 basis points versus last year. Adjusted EPS guidance is set at $10.95 to $11.45, representing an expected increase of 8% to 13%.
  • Capital expenditures for fiscal 2026 are expected to be about $875 million. For Q1 2026, total sales are projected to rise 9% to 11%, with comp sales up 2% to 4%, and adjusted EPS outlook in the range of $1.60 to $1.75.
  • Management indicated, "there may be potential upside to this guidance, and we are positioned to chase the sales trend."

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter adjusted EBIT margin was 12.1%. Adjusted earnings per share in Q4 was $4.99, a 21% increase from the prior year. Gross margin for Q4 increased to 43.7%, up 80 basis points, with a 60 basis point improvement in merchandise margin and 20 basis point reduction in freight expenses. Product sourcing costs were $232 million.
  • SG&A costs in Q4 were 40 basis points lower than last year, driven by store payroll and occupancy leverage. Comparable store inventories were up 12% year-over-year, while reserve inventory was 40% of total, returning to historical norms. The company ended the quarter with $1.2 billion in cash and no ABL borrowings.
  • For the full year, operating margin expanded by 80 basis points, merchandise margin rose by 40 basis points, and 104 net new stores were added.

Q&A

  • Matthew Boss, JPMorgan: Asked about drivers of Q4 sales and the ability to achieve even higher sales. O'Sullivan responded that while pleased with the trend, "our comp growth in Q3 and Q4 was strongest in these specific businesses" less impacted by tariffs, with home and holiday categories underperforming due to deliberate inventory reductions. He added, "if we had more receipts, then for sure, we could have done more business. But those additional sales would have come with unacceptably low margins."
  • Irwin Boruchow, Wells Fargo: Asked about the higher comp guidance for 2026 and its implications. O'Sullivan explained that "raising guidance to 1% to 3% is not a very significant change, it does matter. It gives our merchants a little more open to buy... it's another indicator that we see potential sales upside in 2026."
  • Boruchow followed up on margin guidance for Q1. Wolfe detailed that lower Q1 margin is expected due to lack of tariff anniversary, markdown timing, and new DC start-up costs. "We do expect EBIT margins to increase to varying degrees for each of Q2, Q3 and Q4 to offset the lower operating margin in Q1."
  • Lorraine Maikis, BofA: Asked about tax refunds and inventory strategy. O'Sullivan said, "whether it's a tax refund check or a stimulus check, it puts extra money in our customers' pockets," but expected impact is more muted than 2021's stimulus. Wolfe emphasized, "this was deliberate, and we feel very good about the amount of inventory and the freshness of that inventory."
  • Brooke Roach, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about monthly comp cadence and demographic trends. Wolfe stated, "for November and December combined... comp sales increased mid-single digits," with January also seeing mid-single-digit comps despite storm-related closures. O'Sullivan noted, "comp sales trends in the fourth quarter were very broad-based" across income and ethnic groups.
  • Adrienne Yih-Tennant, Barclays: Sought more color on the elevation strategy and supply chain. O'Sullivan said, "by elevating our assortments, we've been able to drive higher customer perception scores, stronger comp growth in higher-priced buckets... without taking a hit to margin."
  • Mark Altschwager, Baird: Asked about new store pipeline and localization. O'Sullivan expressed optimism, "we are very excited about our new store program and our new store pipeline over the next couple of years," and highlighted localization as "a major opportunity for us."
  • Dana Telsey, Telsey Advisory Group: Questioned margin drivers. Wolfe attributed Q4 margin gains to "an increase in gross margin," with "60 of that 80 basis points came from merchandise margin," and confirmed margin gains are "absolutely sustainable."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was positive, with focus on sales upside, margin sustainability, and strategic clarity, as seen in congratulatory language and follow-up questions about growth levers.
  • Management's sentiment was highly confident and optimistic, especially regarding 2026 prospects, using language such as "bullish" and "very pleased with our results." O'Sullivan repeatedly cited the success of operating adjustments and readiness to pursue sales upside.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone shifted from cautious optimism to overt bullishness, while analysts maintained a constructive but probing approach.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 was raised from a prior range of flat to 2% comp growth to 1% to 3%, reflecting increased confidence in sales trends and inventory positioning.
  • Management's focus shifted from navigating tariff headwinds and weather-related comp volatility in Q3 to emphasizing margin resilience and new growth opportunities in Q4.
  • Analysts' questions transitioned from concerns about relative comp performance and margin trade-offs in Q3 to exploring drivers of outperformance, inventory strategy, and growth initiatives in Q4.
  • Key financial metrics—such as operating margin, earnings per share, and gross margin—demonstrated sequential improvement, with management reiterating the sustainability of recent gains.
  • Strategic priorities evolved from cautious comp planning and margin preservation to aggressive pursuit of sales growth, new store expansion, and localization.

Risks and Concerns

  • Tariffs and their impact on assortment and margins remain a risk, though management now expects less pressure as the industry has largely adjusted.
  • Q1 2026 margin is projected to be lower due to markdown timing, lack of tariff anniversary, and new distribution center start-up costs.
  • The potential for weather disruptions and macroeconomic uncertainty, particularly around tax refund timing and size, were highlighted as factors that could affect near-term sales.
  • Analysts raised questions about competitive dynamics, the sustainability of margin gains, and the effectiveness of localization and supply chain initiatives.

Final Takeaway

Burlington Stores closed fiscal 2025 with robust double-digit sales growth, expanding margins, and strong earnings gains despite tariff headwinds. Management is "bullish" entering 2026, raising comp guidance and targeting aggressive new store growth, while emphasizing that recent margin achievements are sustainable and that the company is well positioned to chase sales upside as market conditions improve.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Pan American discovers new high-grade veins at its largest silver mine
data.description:
Pan American Silver (PAAS) said Thursday that exploration drilling at its La Colorada mine in Mexico resulted in the discovery of at least four new veins with high silver, gold, and base metal grades in the southeastern Candelaria zone.

Drilling identified the Filomena, Nicolasa, Bernardina, and Josefina veins, along with associated contact-related replacement mineralization, the company said, adding that ~40% of drill holes from the campaign returned silver assays exceeding 1,000 g/t.

"With silver assays exceeding 1,000 g/t in 40% of the reported drill holes, many with attendant high base-metal and gold grades, the discovered structures indicate the potential to add to the mineral resources at La Colorada," Senior VP of Exploration and Geology Christopher Emerson said.

The discoveries also support Pan American's (PAAS) revised strategy for a phased development plan at La Colorada focused on higher-grade zones within both the vein system and the skarn deposit, the company said.

Pan American (PAAS) and other precious metals miners traded broadly lower on Thursday as metal prices fell.

data.title: Zillow launches additional up to $1.25B share buyback program
data.description:
Zillow Group's (Z) (ZG) board has authorized an additional up to $1.25B stock repurchase program, the Seattle-based real estate service provider said on Thursday.

Z shares were 2.29% higher at $46.87 around noon, while ZG was up 2.43% to $47.22.

The company is set to buy back class A common stock or class C capital stock under the new program. Now, Zillow has about $1.3B capacity for future share repurchases.

Under the new authorization, the company is set to purchase shares from time to time in the open market or through privately negotiated transactions, or in a manner deemed appropriate by management.

data.title: Seaport Entertainment outlines transition to positive free cash flow with Balloon Museum lease and targets >20% returns on new event space
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Seaport Entertainment Group Inc. (SEG) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Matthew Partridge highlighted major achievements, stating, "we made tremendous progress in 2025 and year-to-date 2026... including generating a 24% year-over-year improvement in our net loss and a 49% year-over-year improvement in our non-GAAP adjusted net loss." He emphasized the sale of 250 Water Street, resulting in "net proceeds of approximately $75 million" and eliminating "$7 million of annual cash burn related to interest expense and carry costs." The closure of the Tin Building and its repositioning through a new lease with Lux Entertainment for the Balloon Museum was described as a "fundamental repositioning" expected to transition the asset to positive free cash flow and potentially improve annual EBITDA by over $22 million. Partridge also noted the signing of a 10-year lease with a Brooklyn-based arts and hospitality concept and the upcoming opening of Sadie's, a new 400-seat, 1,000-person open container district restaurant.

  • Partridge detailed plans to expand Pier 17 event space to "more than 40,000 square feet across 3 floors" and projected "long-term unlevered cash-on-cash returns above 20% with an estimated payback period under 5 years."

  • The Las Vegas Aviators' championship and strong ticket sales momentum were cited, with Partridge stating, "group and season ticket sales are pacing ahead of last year." He also announced Board approval for a "$150 million shelf registration statement and a $50 million stock repurchase program," providing capital flexibility.

  • CFO Lenah Elaiwat reported, "net loss attributable to common stockholders was $36.9 million and $116.7 million, respectively, representing a year-over-year improvement of 11% for Q4 2025 and 24% for the full year 2025." She added, "Non-GAAP adjusted net loss... was $17.5 million for the fourth quarter of 2025 and $54.1 million for the full year, representing improvements of 9% and 49%, respectively."

Outlook

  • Partridge indicated the company expects "continued margin improvement in 2026 across the entire Las Vegas operation."

  • The expanded event space at Pier 17 is projected to generate "long-term unlevered cash-on-cash returns above 20% with an estimated payback period under 5 years." More details are to be provided on the next earnings call.

  • For capital allocation, Elaiwat stated, "we expect around another $70 million to $90 million" to reach stabilization of current projects, maintaining a target range of "$100 million to $125 million."

Financial Results

  • Total consolidated revenues for Q4 2025 were $29.5 million, a 7% increase year-over-year compared to pro forma Q4 2024. Full year 2025 revenues were $130.4 million, essentially flat year-over-year on a pro forma basis.

  • Hospitality segment Q4 revenues declined 23% on a pro forma basis, attributed to Tin Building performance and non-repeating events. Hospitality consolidated adjusted EBITDA for Q4 improved by $11 million year-over-year, largely due to a prior year impairment charge.

  • Entertainment segment Q4 revenues increased 68% year-over-year, driven by internalizing Enchant operations in Las Vegas. Entertainment operating EBITDA increased 18% for the quarter.

  • Landlord segment Q4 rental revenue rose 14% year-over-year on a pro forma basis, with consolidated adjusted EBITDA declining by $10.1 million, mainly due to a $7 million write-down of 250 Water Street.

  • Full year G&A expenses were $42.8 million, a 32% improvement. Year-end long-term debt stood at $100.4 million. Cash, restricted cash, and equivalents at year-end were $87 million, pro forma $163 million post-250 Water Street sale.

Q&A

  • Matthew Erdner, JonesTrading: Asked about the deployment of the $163 million cash balance and capital allocation priorities. Elaiwat responded, "our expectation for everything we've announced plus the existing vacancy to get to stabilization is another... around another $70 million to $90 million." Partridge added, "we're going to look at a lot of different things... we could potentially look at other assets similar to the Seaport... or be opportunistic and utilize the buyback program."

  • Erdner asked about internal hurdles for event space returns. Partridge noted, "the events business is a much better margin-oriented business... if we can find things that complement the existing skill set, that's going to improve the flow-through of whatever we allocate capital to."

  • Erdner inquired about remaining Seaport space and EBITDA growth potential. Partridge said, "we've got a little over 50,000 square feet left... 1/3 of that is probably restaurant-oriented space... pulling some of the food and beverage supply out with the closure of the Tin Building and positioning it to Balloon Museum is going to help all the other F&B."

  • Erdner asked about special events programming. Partridge highlighted, "Sadie's is definitely going to be a unique asset for us to program around... FIFA and the World Cup are going to be a unique event this year... America’s 250-year anniversary... we're going to do a lot of programming around cultural events and sporting events."

  • Erdner queried about G&A run rate. Elaiwat said, "Q4 is our new reference point, and we're continuing to try and refine that."

  • Patrick Stadelhofer, Kahn Brothers Group: Questioned buyback criteria. Partridge: "we won't put out any public comments related to parameters or timing... we use them opportunistically."

  • Stadelhofer asked about the Balloon Museum and Meow Wolf relationship. Partridge responded, "the Balloon Museum is definitely complementary... they're both ticketed experiences... having them both open down here really gives the local population... a full day opportunity to spend at the Seaport."

  • Stadelhofer asked about monetizing the apartment building at 85 South Street. Partridge stated, "it is cash flowing... almost 100% leased... the interest has definitely ramped up."

  • Stadelhofer queried Vegas vs. New York focus. Partridge said, "in Las Vegas, we've got a phenomenal facility... things like Enchant are where we can add a lot of value... live sports is a great business... if and when somebody has an interest in the team, we'll always listen."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts demonstrated a positive, constructive tone, congratulating management and seeking clarity on capital allocation, event-driven growth, and operational improvements. Questions focused on deployment of cash, event space returns, buyback strategy, and asset monetization.

  • Management maintained a confident and forward-looking tone throughout, emphasizing flexibility and optionality in capital allocation while referencing significant improvements and new initiatives. Partridge used phrases like "I'm confident we'll continue building on the strong momentum" and "we're going to look at a lot of different things."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management tone was more assertive and detailed regarding strategic direction and capital deployment. Analyst sentiment shifted from cautious optimism to a more constructive, opportunity-focused engagement, reflecting increased confidence in management’s execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Strategic focus shifted from stabilization and assessment in Q3 to active repositioning, asset sales, and new partnerships in Q4. The closure and repurposing of the Tin Building into the Balloon Museum and the sale of 250 Water Street marked significant events this quarter.

  • Guidance language evolved from a focus on "opportunistic" capital allocation and operational optimization to explicit targets for cash-on-cash returns and capex deployment.

  • Analysts' focus expanded from profitability levers and tenant mix to detailed questions on cash deployment, buybacks, and event programming.

  • Key metrics moved from improvements in same-store revenue and cost structure to explicit year-over-year improvements in net loss and non-GAAP adjusted net loss, and a shift from capital preservation to proactive investment and balance sheet strengthening.

  • Management’s confidence increased, supported by successful asset sales, progress on cost optimization, and new programming initiatives.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged the historical challenges of the Tin Building and the need for a "fundamental repositioning" to achieve long-term sustainability.

  • The sale of 250 Water Street took longer than anticipated, highlighting process risks in asset monetization.

  • Exposure to variable costs and the importance of margin control in Las Vegas operations were mentioned, with ongoing efforts to refine execution and expense management.

  • Analyst questions reflected concerns around sustainable EBITDA generation, deployment of excess cash, and the timing and criteria for share repurchases.

Final Takeaway

Seaport Entertainment Group closed a transformative quarter, highlighted by the sale of 250 Water Street, the closure and repositioning of the Tin Building, and new partnerships to drive diversified programming at the Seaport. Management emphasized improved financial performance, strengthened liquidity, and a robust pipeline of event and hospitality initiatives, with a clear focus on deploying capital for high-return opportunities and maintaining flexibility for future growth and shareholder returns.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Airline stocks lose altitude amid Middle East flight cancellations and fuel surge
data.description:
Escalating tensions between the United States and Iran are rippling through global aviation markets, weighing heavily on airline stocks.

Heightened security risks across parts of the Middle East have prompted many carriers to suspend or reroute flights through the region, disrupting schedules and adding operational uncertainty. 

At the same time, a sharp rise in oil (CL1:COM) prices has intensified cost pressures for airlines, as fuel remains one of the industry’s largest expenses. The combination of higher operating expenses and geopolitical risk has sparked a broad sell-off across the sector in recent trading sessions. 

Over the past five days, shares of major carriers have broadly declined as investors reassess the near-term outlook for global air travel.

Listed below are the 20 largest global airline companies by market capitalization and their respective five-day stock performance. 

  1. Thai Airways International (TAWNF), -17.1%
  2. Alaska Air Group (ALK), -13.1%
  3. Singapore Airlines Limited (SINGF), -11.3%
  4. Qantas Airways Limited (QUBSF), -11.1%
  5. United Airlines Holdings (UAL), -8.2% 
  6. Southwest Airlines (LUV), -8.0%
  7. LATAM Airlines Group S.A. (LTM), -7.9%
  8. Japan Airlines (JAPSY), -7.8%
  9. SkyWest (SKYW), -7.3% 
  10. International Consolidated Airlines Group S.A. (ICAGY), -7.1%
  11. Delta Air Lines (DAL), -6.6% 
  12. Ryanair Holdings (RYAAY), -6.1%

  20. ANA Holdings (ALNPY), -0.2%

Airline ETFs: (JETS), (JETU), and (JETD).

data.title: Netflix buys Ben Affleck's firm InterPositive that develops AI tools to make films
data.description:
Streaming giant Netflix (NFLX) said Thursday it has acquired Ben Affleck's filmmaking tech company InterPositive for an undisclosed sum.

InterPositive develops AI-powered tools and consists of a small team of engineers, researchers, and creatives. Affleck will also join the company as a senior advisor.

The move comes in the aftermath of the bidding war for Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) against Paramount Skydance (PSKY), in which the David Ellison-led media company emerged victorious and agreed to pay Netflix a $2.8B termination fee. 

Netflix is expected to invest up to $20B this year in films and series and to support new studio deals, live events, and gaming.
